metadata.title: Guideline on clinical investigation of medicinal products for the treatment of acute heart failure

rules.rule_text: The preferred primary endpoint is mortality (CV mortality or all-cause mortality), especially at 30 or 60 days.
rules.verbatim_quote: The preferred primary endpoint is mortality (CV mortality or all-cause mortality), especially at 30 or 60 days, as the treatment for AHF is often short term administration (≤7 days) of the investigational agent.
rules.exceptions: For products with short term administration (≤7 days), symptomatic improvement might be acceptable as primary endpoint.

rules.rule_text: If CV mortality is chosen as the primary endpoint, all-cause mortality must be a secondary endpoint.
rules.verbatim_quote: If cardiovascular mortality is chosen as the primary endpoint, all-cause mortality should be a secondary endpoint. It is important that applicants should be prepared to inform about longer term outcome especially all-cause mortality even when short term outcomes (e.g., symptoms or clinical status) are used as primary endpoints.

rules.rule_text: When symptoms are chosen as primary endpoints in pivotal trials, evidence must be provided that longer term outcomes such as mortality are not adversely influenced.
rules.verbatim_quote: When symptoms either singly or as a composite (with clinical status) are chosen as primary endpoints in pivotal trials, it will be necessary to provide evidence that longer term outcomes such as mortality are not adversely influenced.

rules.rule_text: Symptomatic improvement as primary endpoint is only acceptable for products with short term administration (7 days or less). Symptoms must be assessed under standardised conditions at specified time points.
rules.verbatim_quote: For products with short term administration (≤7 days), symptomatic improvement might be acceptable as primary endpoint. Symptoms should be carefully assessed under standardised conditions and at specified time points.

rules.rule_text: Change in background therapy alone cannot be accepted as a surrogate for symptomatic improvement.
rules.verbatim_quote: Change in background therapy alone cannot be accepted as a surrogate for symptomatic improvement.

rules.rule_text: Dyspnoea assessments must demonstrate persistent improvement, with timing usually at 6 hours and thereafter until at least 24 hours after initiation and termination of investigative therapy.
rules.verbatim_quote: Timing of the assessments to demonstrate persistent improvement (usually at 6 hours and thereafter, until at least 24 hours after initiation and termination of investigative therapy) is an important aspect that should be addressed and specified in the study protocol.

rules.rule_text: Improvement in dyspnoea should be supported by improvement in clinical signs of congestion or global clinical status change.
rules.verbatim_quote: Improvement in dyspnoea should be supported by improvement in clinical signs of congestion [3] or global clinical status change (see also sections 4.1.3, 4.2.7 and 4.2.8).

rules.rule_text: The dyspnoea measurement method must be justified and defined a priori.
rules.verbatim_quote: Any method chosen should be justified and defined a priori.

rules.rule_text: Responder analysis discriminatory value must be defined a priori to ensure the analysis is sensitive or reflective of changes in pathophysiology.
rules.verbatim_quote: their discriminatory value should be defined a priori in order to ensure that the responder analysis is sensitive or reflective of changes in pathophysiology.

rules.rule_text: Awareness of haemodynamic effects (e.g., PCWP) during early phase studies must be avoided when symptoms are evaluated as endpoints, to prevent unblinding bias.
rules.verbatim_quote: When the effects on pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) or other haemodynamic parameters are evaluated in early phase studies, investigator or sponsor awareness of such effects may jeopardise blinding and thus influence the evaluation of symptoms. This awareness should be avoided, especially when these haemodynamic parameters are included as endpoints (co-primary or other) in the same (early phase) study.

rules.rule_text: Composite endpoints must consist of objective clinical events with directional concordance. Diverse and disparate measures are not acceptable as components.
rules.verbatim_quote: Composite endpoints should ideally consist of objective clinical events and the components should demonstrate directional concordance. ... Diverse and disparate measures would not be acceptable as components of a composite endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Haemodynamic measurements are not routinely included in Phase III trials and their use as sole primary endpoints in Phase II-III studies is not recommended.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is recognised that haemodynamic measurements are not routinely included in Phase III trials and their use in pivotal studies is not encouraged. ... Use of haemodynamic measurements as sole primary endpoints in phase II-III studies is not recommended.
rules.exceptions: Useful as secondary endpoints or co-primary endpoints in early phase trials only.

rules.rule_text: Reduction of PCWP has not been proven to be a useful or representative surrogate endpoint for clinical outcome or survival.
rules.verbatim_quote: Reduction of PCWP has not been proven to be useful or representative and is not an acceptable surrogate endpoint for clinical outcome or survival.

rules.rule_text: When dyspnoea is the primary endpoint in short-term trials, mortality (preferably all-cause) should be a secondary endpoint with similar time points of evaluation.
rules.verbatim_quote: When the primary endpoint is dyspnoea in the short-term trials (days to weeks), mortality (preferably all cause) should be a secondary endpoint with similar time points of evaluation as when mortality is evaluated as a primary end point (for example, in hospital, 14 days, 30days or 60 days).

rules.rule_text: Hospital readmissions used as secondary endpoints require standardised definitions and criteria included in the protocol, and may require central adjudication.
rules.verbatim_quote: When any of the above is included as a secondary endpoint, their definitions and criteria used for evaluation should be standardised and included in the protocol. Such events may require central adjudication.

rules.rule_text: Heart failure hospitalisation (HFH) endpoint must be objectively defined with efforts to differentiate HFH from non-CV co-morbidity hospitalisations.
rules.verbatim_quote: There is a need to objectively define HFH if it were to be used as an endpoint. Efforts must be put in place to differentiate HFH from those due to non-cardiovascular co-morbidities as well due to a CV cause but not primarily due to heart failure must be noted.

rules.rule_text: If worsening heart failure (WHF) is used as an endpoint, clear pre-specified criteria must be defined in the protocol with objective parameters, and events may need to be adjudicated.
rules.verbatim_quote: If WHF is used as a secondary end point or as a part of a composite primary end point, clear criteria would be necessary to define this event to reduce regional or investigator variability and events may need to be adjudicated. The criteria for WHF should be pre-specified in the protocol to achieve consistency. They should be clinically relevant and preferably include objective parameters such as need for mechanical ventilation including positive pressure ventilation and use of pre-defined rescue therapy.

rules.rule_text: BNP and NT-pro-BNP cannot serve as primary endpoints in pivotal trials, either singly or in combinations. Their surrogate value is unproven.
rules.verbatim_quote: In pivotal trials, neither of these peptides will serve as primary endpoints (singly or in combinations) based on the current level of scientific knowledge and evidence base. Their value as surrogate endpoint is unproven.
rules.exceptions: Can be used as exploratory/tertiary endpoints and for patient selection/inclusion criteria.

rules.rule_text: BNP/NT-pro-BNP cut-off values for inclusion/exclusion criteria must be defined a priori.
rules.verbatim_quote: Assessment of natriuretic peptides (BNP or NT pro-BNP) at present is most useful for their negative predictive value in the diagnosis of AHF and could be used as entry criteria (i.e. for inclusion/exclusion of patients) provided that cut off values for both BNP and NT pro-BNP are defined "a priori".

rules.rule_text: BNP/NT-pro-BNP cannot be recommended for stratification or prognosis at this time. Use is limited to exploratory/tertiary endpoints.
rules.verbatim_quote: Use of BNP or NT pro-BNP for stratification or for prognosis cannot be recommended at this point in time. However, their levels may be used as exploratory/ tertiary end points for evaluating the effect of the intervention.

rules.rule_text: Renal function indices do not have sufficient evidence base to function as primary endpoints in confirmatory trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: These parameters do not have sufficient evidence base to function as primary endpoints at this point in time in confirmatory trials.
rules.exceptions: Can be used as stratification parameters and/or secondary endpoints.

rules.rule_text: Patients must be differentiated between HFrEF (EF <40%) and HFpEF (EF >50%), with consistent definitions used throughout the development programme. If both included, stratified randomisation is recommended.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is necessary to differentiate patients with systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction <40%; HFrEF) and those with preserved systolic function (ejection fraction >50%, HFpEF)... If both HFrEF and HFpEF groups are included in the same trial, a stratified randomisation is recommended.

rules.rule_text: AHF due to ACS and ADHF may need to be studied separately. If different categories are included in the same trial, stratified randomisation with planned sub-group analysis and evaluation of directional concordance and consistency of effects is necessary.
rules.verbatim_quote: AHF due to ACS and ADHF may need to be studied separately. If patients from different categories are included in the same single trial, stratified randomisation, planned sub-group analysis with evaluation of directional concordance and consistency of effects might be necessary.

rules.rule_text: The trial database must include a representative number of patients >65 years and >75 years in confirmatory studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: the database should include a representative number of patients >65 years and >75 years in the therapeutic confirmatory studies (see also Section 7.3 Cardiac events [including myocardial injury]).

rules.rule_text: Adequate numbers of patients with moderate renal dysfunction must be included or investigated in the clinical development programme.
rules.verbatim_quote: adequate numbers of those with moderate renal dysfunction should be included or investigated in the clinical development programme as a minimum.

rules.rule_text: Phase III studies must be double blind and randomised.
rules.verbatim_quote: Phase III studies are expected to be double blind and randomised. The absence of double blinding may compromise the interpretation particularly of symptom-based studies where subjective elements may dominate.

rules.rule_text: Placebo-controlled studies are required for add-on therapy to current conventional treatment or for new therapeutic classes. Patients must receive appropriate background therapy per clinical guidelines.
rules.verbatim_quote: Placebo controlled studies are required if the new medicinal product is intended as add-on therapy to current conventional treatment and belongs to a new therapeutic class or to a therapeutic class which has not previously been considered for the treatment of AHF. Sponsors should ensure that patients receive appropriate background therapy in accordance with clinical guidelines.
rules.exceptions: Absence of placebo-controlled studies will need to be justified.

rules.rule_text: For products in an existing therapeutic class, a double blind, randomised, active comparator controlled study against another licensed product of the same class is expected.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the new medicinal product belongs to an existing therapeutic class, a double blind, randomised, active comparator controlled study against another licensed product of the same therapeutic class is expected.

rules.rule_text: Non-inferiority trials against agents without an approved AHF indication may not be sufficient for approval. Superiority should preferably be established, and a placebo arm should be included for assay sensitivity.
rules.verbatim_quote: In these instances, the trial should preferably aim to establish superiority and non-inferiority trials might not be sufficient for approval. When a hypothesis of non-inferiority is the selected approach, underestimation of any difference between treatments should be avoided and an adequate demonstration of assay sensitivity must be ensured. Inclusion of a placebo arm would serve this purpose.

rules.rule_text: Dose selection for pivotal studies must be based on haemodynamics, signs and symptoms, patient reported outcomes, and tolerability -- not on haemodynamic parameters alone.
rules.verbatim_quote: Choosing doses based on haemodynamic parameters only is often a risky strategy as the link between changes in haemodynamics and outcome measures may be tenuous. Therefore, the choice of the dose in pivotal studies is expected to be based both on haemodynamics, signs and symptoms, patient reported outcomes and tolerability.

rules.rule_text: In active comparator trials, appropriate licensed doses of the comparator must be used. Doses not authorised in the EU require strong justification and biological rationale.
rules.verbatim_quote: In active comparator trials, appropriate licensed doses of the comparator should be used. Use of doses not authorised in the EU for the comparator might not be acceptable and would need to have strong justification and biological rationale.

rules.rule_text: Concomitant therapy must be optimised and predefined in the study protocol. Changes in concomitant medications must be carefully documented, and unscheduled changes should be minimised.
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of concomitant therapy should in all cases be optimised and predefined in the study protocol. ... Changes in concomitant medications or dose of concomitant medications could be useful information as part of the overall evaluation of improvement and should be carefully documented. Unscheduled or unplanned changes during the study should be kept to a minimum.

rules.rule_text: The safety database must be adequate to exclude any potential increase in mortality. Even for symptomatic benefit claims, mortality data for the hospitalised period, end of 30 days, and over six months must be available.
rules.verbatim_quote: Even if the claim is made for symptomatic benefit only, mortality data for the hospitalised period, end of 30 days period and over six months are expected to be available in the database in order to exclude the possibility of any deleterious effect.

rules.rule_text: Evidence for exclusion of mortality harm: upper bound of 95% two-sided confidence interval for hazard ratio should be below 1.8 when HR is approximately 1.
rules.verbatim_quote: evidence for exclusion of harm in AHF patients using a pooled approach could be an upper bound of the 95%, two sided confidence interval below 1.8 in the event that HR is approximately 1.
rules.exceptions: For active comparators, thresholds may differ and will need a priori discussion with regulatory agencies.

rules.rule_text: Cardiac safety evaluation must include 12-lead ECGs, Holter monitoring, troponin measurement for myocardial injury, and monitoring for QTc prolongation during early drug development.
rules.verbatim_quote: Evaluation should include 12-lead ECGs and Holter monitoring. Measurement of myocardial injury before discharge by troponin or other suitable biomarker may be of value as a safety measure. It is important to carefully monitor for any possibility of QTc prolongation (alteration in cardiac repolarisation) in addition to evaluation of QT/QTc, during early drug development.
